header_levels.note: Section files must start at h2. h1 triggers normalization in Step 6b. h2 count > 1 means subsections were over-promoted.

gotcha_clusters.note: Cluster count scales with repo complexity. Use distinct subsystems discovered during investigation minus 1, with a floor of 3 for small repos.

module_h3.note: Each investigated module must have its own ### in the Module Index.

CRITICAL.definition: Active data loss, security vulnerability, or silent corruption that affects production behavior NOW — not hypothetically
examples: Dead message routing (messages sent but never received)
Unwired safety checks (validation defined but never called)
Silent data truncation on write path
CRITICAL.calibration_note: If >5% of gotchas are CRITICAL, review each against this definition. Most 'could cause problems' gotchas are HIGH, not CRITICAL. CRITICAL means the bug exists today and is actively harmful.

HIGH.definition: Behavioral surprise that will cause bugs during common modification tasks — wrong assumptions, missing error handling, implicit ordering dependencies
examples: Method returns None on error instead of raising (callers assume non-None)
Retry logic defined but unreachable due to early return
Two models represent same entity with divergent field sets
Singleton lacks thread safety in concurrent context
HIGH.calibration_note: HIGH gotchas cause bugs when you change code nearby. They are dormant until touched.

MEDIUM.definition: Confusing or misleading code that wastes developer time but doesn't cause incorrect behavior in production
examples: Misleading variable name (e.g., 'cache' that doesn't cache)
Overly broad exception catch that hides useful errors during development
MEDIUM.calibration_note: MEDIUM gotchas slow you down but don't break production. If removing the gotcha would change runtime behavior, it's at least HIGH.
